questions.text: I do not automatically pick up on the difference between an invitation and a formality.
I rarely feel emotionally overwhelmed, even during genuinely difficult times.
I often sense that there is a conversation happening beneath the surface conversation.
I believe that a leader who does not listen loses the group's trust.
I systematically review and update my plans as new information becomes available.
I feel that every personal indulgence should be weighed against what it could do for someone else.
I prefer that new initiatives come from a collective mandate.
I believe that the moral weight I assign to others' pain is the foundation on which all of my other ethical commitments rest.
I value adaptability more than predictability in how I approach my day.
I find that I am able to make hard choices about resource allocation without being influenced by the emotional intensity of individual cases.
I hold the view that my emotional stability is not indifference — it's simply a different calibration.
I find that celebrations and happy occasions fill me with a joy so strong it's almost painful.
I tend to take what people say at face value.
I hold the view that remaining rationally centered during emotional upheaval is a capacity that supports not only my own well-being but the well-being of everyone I work alongside.
I see leadership as a core part of my identity.
I believe my personal fulfillment should not depend on what I do for other people.
I like to try a different approach each time, even if the old way worked.
I believe that personal success is a valid and worthy life aim.
